name: 'Stop Destroying Videogames' Citizens' Initiative — Commission response
status: Commission declined to legislate; industry code of conduct promised by end of 2026
summary: 1,294,188 verified EU citizens demanded that games remain playable after publisher support ends. On June 16, 2026 the European Commission said it cannot propose such a law, citing intellectual property rights, and instead committed to an industry code-of-conduct process and 'raising awareness of existing consumer rights'. In other words: transparency is all you get. This app is that transparency.
whatYouCanDo: The fight moved to the Digital Fairness Act. The movement is pushing to get game-preservation duties written into it — public consultations are where citizen voices count.

name: Protect Our Games Act (AB 1921)
status: Passed State Assembly; in Senate committee as of June 2026
summary: Would require publishers selling in California to disclose planned end-of-support and to leave games in a playable state (or refund) when servers shut down. Passed the Assembly in 2026 and moved to the Senate — the most concrete legislative threat to the kill-switch business model anywhere in the US.
whatYouCanDo: California residents: tell your state senator you support AB 1921.

name: AB 2426 — digital 'purchase' disclosure law
summary: Storefronts may no longer tell Californians they are 'buying' a game unless they disclose it's only a revocable license. This is why Steam's checkout now admits you're buying a license. It changed the label, not the deal — but it made the deal visible.

name: The Crew shutdown class action vs. Ubisoft
summary: After Ubisoft killed The Crew and revoked its licenses, California players sued. Ubisoft's defense: buyers received only a limited license and 'never owned' the game. In 2025 the court let key claims proceed — including that unexpired in-game currency functions like a gift card that can't legally expire. The case is live precedent for what 'buy' means.

name: 'Require videogame publishers to keep games they have sold in a working state' petition
summary: The UK petition passed the debate threshold; the government responded that existing consumer law suffices and it has no plans to change the rules. UK players remain covered only by general consumer-protection law that has never been tested against a game shutdown.

name: Digital Fairness Act
status: In legislative preparation; the movement's next battleground
summary: The EU's upcoming consumer-protection overhaul. After the Commission declined to act on the citizens' initiative directly, Stop Killing Games is working to have playability and end-of-life duties included here — 'we are in a position to pass legislation on this even without the Commission's blessing', per founder Ross Scott.
whatYouCanDo: EU citizens: respond to Digital Fairness Act consultations and contact your MEPs.
